Name: informationtheoreticmodelcompressionbounds
Title: Information-Theoretic Bounds for Model Compression in Deep Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by deriving tight information-theoretic bounds on the rate-distortion tradeoff for model compression, we can identify the optimal compression schemes that minimize the population risk while maintaining predictive sufficiency.
Related Work: Previous work has explored the relationship between model compression and population risk improvement using information-theoretic tools (Bu et al., 2019; Bu et al., 2021). These studies have shown that model compression can reduce generalization error, but they do not provide tight bounds on the tradeoff between compression rate and distortion. Additionally, while rate-distortion theory has been applied to model compression (Isik et al., 2021; Nokleby et al., 2015), the focus has been on specific compression techniques rather than deriving fundamental limits. Our proposal aims to fill this gap by providing a comprehensive framework for understanding the optimal compression rate for deep neural networks.
Abstract: We propose to derive information-theoretic bounds on the rate-distortion tradeoff for model compression in deep neural networks. By framing model compression as a lossy source coding problem, we seek to identify the optimal compression schemes that minimize the population risk while maintaining predictive sufficiency. Our approach involves deriving achievable and converse bounds on the compression rate, characterizing the distortion-rate function, and proposing practical algorithms to approach these bounds. We will evaluate our methods using both synthetic and real datasets, comparing the achieved compression rates and population risks against existing compression techniques. This work aims to provide a theoretical foundation for understanding the limits of model compression and to guide the development of more efficient compression algorithms.
Experiments: Derive achievable and converse bounds on the rate-distortion tradeoff for model compression in deep neural networks.
Characterize the distortion-rate function for various compression techniques, including pruning, quantization, and clustering.
Develop practical algorithms to approach the derived bounds, such as iterative pruning with regularization based on Hessian-weighted K-means clustering.
Evaluate the performance of the proposed algorithms on synthetic datasets to validate the theoretical bounds.
Conduct experiments on real-world datasets, such as ImageNet and CIFAR-10, to compare the achieved compression rates and population risks against existing methods.
Analyze the tradeoff between compression rate and task performance, and assess the generalization properties of the compressed models.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The derived bounds may be tight only under certain assumptions about the model and data distribution, which may not hold in practice.
The practical algorithms developed may require significant computational resources, especially for large-scale models and datasets.
The theoretical analysis may be challenging due to the complexity of deep neural networks and the non-convex nature of the optimization problems involved.
The performance of the proposed methods may be sensitive to hyperparameters, requiring careful tuning to achieve optimal results.
